**1.  What a counterfactual “If … then …” really says**

A counterfactual of the form  

\[
\text{If }A\text{ had occurred, then }B\text{ would have occurred}
\]

is not a simple “if‑then” statement about the actual world.  It is a claim about **a possible world that is like the actual world except for the stipulated change** (the antecedent \(A\)).  The claim is true only if, in every such “closest” possible world, the consequent \(B\) also holds.  Philosophers and AI researchers formalise this
